purpose: Test whether student can solve a 1st-factor unknown — the easier unknown position ("how many groups?") allowing skip counting by the 2nd factor. This is the CRITICAL coverage problem per the Lesson Phase 1st-Factor Coverage Note. Language prompt included alongside symbolic equation, matching Lesson 3.3 scaffolding method.
visual: Equal Groups with Pictures in Construction Mode. Equation Builder with pre-filled template: `☐ × 5 = 15`. Language prompt: "\*\*\_ stacks of 5 equals 15 total." Construction area starts empty. \+/- group control available (each new group pre-set to 5 items per the equation's known factor). Running total display: "Total: 0" MC options: `[2] [3] [4] [5]`.
guide: "How many stacks of 5 make 15? Pick the number."
answer_rationale:
- 3 \= Correct (3 × 5 \= 15; skip-count by 5s: 5, 10, 15 — three counts)
	- 2 \= Skip-count error (stopped at 10, thought 2 was enough)
	- 4 \= Skip-count error (counted one too many)
	- 5 \= Confused unknown with the visible factor (sees "5" in the equation, places "5")
remediation_note: Have student skip-count by 5s and count how many times they count to reach 15 (5, 10, 15 = 3 times). Distractor 2 = stopped at 10; Distractor 4 = counted one too many; Distractor 5 = confused unknown with the visible factor.
visual_on_correct: 3 stacks of 5 items appear, building one at a time (same confirmation pattern as Lesson 3.3).
design_note: Language prompt ("\*\*\_ stacks of 5 equals 15 total") is essential scaffolding — it was taught this way in Lesson 3.3 and must appear the same way in Exit Check. MC keeps computation accessible. The value 3 × 5 \= 15 appeared in Lesson 1.5 as a full equation build (standard form, all numbers given, bags context). The Exit Check tests a fundamentally different skill: identifying a missing FACTOR from a pre-filled equation in a variety context. Different cognitive type (IDENTIFY vs CREATE), different unknown position (1st factor vs none), different context (stacks vs bags). This is not recall — it's transfer.
